title: Internet Infrastructures: 1998 Practice Summary
author: Aberdeen Group
abstract: Aberdeen Group's 1998 analysis of the Internet Infrastructure market, defining the four-component architecture (Application Development Environment, Application Runtime Environment, Service Middleware, and ISP Services) and introducing the 'Internet Object Computing' (IOC) framework. The report assesses the Java vs. Microsoft COM+/DNA battle, the role of CORBA and LDAP, the maturity of EJB and distributed computing standards, and provides competitive profiles of eight major vendors: Borland/Inprise, HP, IBM, Microsoft, Netscape, Novell, Oracle, and Sybase.
importance_rationale: Institutional documentation of internet infrastructure at a critical architectural decision point in 1998; captures the Java vs. COM+ platform war and emergence of enterprise web architecture with notable analytical depth.
relevance_rationale: Internet infrastructure frameworks (microservices, containers, cloud APIs) are direct descendants of the IOC/EJB/CORBA concepts described; LDAP remains the directory standard; the analysis of platform lock-in vs. open standards is perennially relevant.
prescience_rationale: Key predictions—LDAP becoming entrenched as directory standard, proprietary directories heading for extinction, Java's enterprise adoption, Internet outsourcing growth, Microsoft's scalability challenges with NT-tied DNA strategy—all proved accurate.
